item.text: {"summary":"Live search suggests a distinct physical representation based on how redshifted spectral emission lines move through SDSS ugriz filters, complementing prior color, SED-continuum, sky, and reliability hypotheses.","hypotheses":[{"title":"Redshifted Line Filter Footprint Features","summary":"Encode where important stellar, galaxy, and quasar spectral lines would fall inside the SDSS ugriz filter set at the observed redshift.","feature_family":"physical_filter_line_features","feature_strategy":"Keep raw ugriz magnitudes, redshift, alpha/delta, and one-hot spectral_type/galaxy_population, then add target-free spectral-line/filter geometry features. Use fixed rest-frame wavelengths for lines such as Ly-alpha 1216, C IV 1549, C III] 1909, Mg II 2798, [O II] 3727, H-beta 4861, [O III] 5007, H-alpha 6563, and Ca H/K around 3934/3969 Angstrom. For each row compute observed_line_wavelength = rest_wavelength * (1 + clipped_redshift). Using fixed SDSS ugriz effective wavelengths and approximate band widths, add normalized distance from each observed line to each band center, soft Gaussian band-affinity weights, nearest-band id, min distance to a filter gap/edge, line-visible flags within the optical ugriz range, per-band summed line-affinity totals, blue-line versus red-line affinity balances, and interactions between summed line-affinity per band and that band magnitude or adjacent colors. Do not learn thresholds or line weights from class labels.","baseline_model_panel":"Use one comparable simple panel: a class-weighted regularized multinomial linear model on scaled numeric plus one-hot categorical features, a class-balanced random-forest or extra-trees model, and one class-balanced gradient-boosted tree model such as CatBoost with balanced class weights or LightGBM with class_weight='balanced' if available.","model_panel_rationale":"The linear model checks whether fixed physical line-position features add mostly additive signal, while tree models can exploit discontinuities when emission lines cross filters or gaps; the panel is diverse enough to measure feature value without turning the hypothesis into model tuning.","validation_strategy":"Use 5-fold StratifiedKFold with balanced accuracy, preserving identical folds and class-weight handling across the baseline panel; inspect per-class scores because the feature should mainly help QSO and emission-line galaxy separation.","materialization_hint":"Materialize constants for rest-frame line wavelengths and SDSS filter centers/widths, generate all features from train+test covariates without target labels, one-hot the two categorical columns, scale only for the linear model, and log feature count plus top feature importances or coefficients from the simple models.","expected_signal":"If useful, CV balanced accuracy should improve most for QSO versus STAR/GALAXY confusions, and tree feature importances should surface line-near-band, line-near-gap, or per-band line-affinity aggregates rather than only raw redshift.","novelty_confidence":"medium","risk":"Some signal may overlap with existing redshift, color, and analytic SED-template features, and the synthetic data may not preserve detailed filter-gap physics. Approximate filter widths and line constants could be too crude, so this may behave like another nonlinear redshift transform rather than a genuinely new representation.","sources":["https://arxiv.org/abs/0911.5700","https://arxiv.org/abs/astro-ph/0408504","https://arxiv.org/abs/1002.3701","https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sloan_Digital_Survey"]}]}
